content:
Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes Chia-Ming Chang a,1 , Qipeng Zhao b,c,1, Shing Bor Chen a,* a Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, 117585, Singaporeb State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, Chinac Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, China

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Thin-film composite membranesReverse osmosis15-Crown-5Solvent activationDesalination

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: A B S T R A C T This study presents an innovative approach to enhance the separation performance and stability of thin-film composite (TFC) reverse osmosis (RO) membranes through post-treatment by inserting 15-crown-5 (CE15) as molecular supports, assisted by methanol. By varying the CE15 concentration (0–4 wt%), the physicochemical properties of the membranes can be regulated with significantly improved separation performance. Compre­hensive characterizations reveal that an optimal CE15 concentration of 1 wt% increases the water permeance by 148 % (from 1.86 to 4.61 LMH bar-1) while maintaining a high salt rejection of 98.9 %. Additionally, the chelation of CE15 with Li+ or Na+ further enhances the membrane’s structural robustness, ensuring long-term stability. Over a 72-h period, the treated membranes exhibit only a 3.4 % reduction in water permeance, compared to a 15.8 % decline observed for the untreated membranes. This facile post-treatment method offers a scalable and effective solution to improve the permeability, selectivity, and durability of TFC membranes, pre­senting a promising advancement for desalination and water treatment applications.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: 1. Introduction The 21st century has witnessed an unprecedented surge in water demand, driven by increasing industrial activities and exponential population growth, leading to a global water crisis of alarming pro­portions [1–5]. This pressing issue has catalyzed extensive research on advanced water purification methodologies, with a particular focus on membrane-based separation techniques, lauded for their operational ease and energy conservation [6–13]. Within the spectrum of filtration technologies, several approaches have gained attractions for their versatility in tackling diverse water treatment challenges, including microfiltration (MF) [14–18], ultrafiltration (UF) [19–23], nano­filtration (NF) [24–29], and reverse osmosis (RO) [30–34]. Among these, RO has emerged as a transformative force in water purification. Utilizing semi-permeable barriers, RO facilitates the passage of water molecules while impeding solutes, yielding high-purity water. This capability has established RO as a formidable solution across various water treatment scenarios, ranging from seawater desalination to industrial effluent management [30–42]. In the domain of RO technology, TFC polyamide membranes have garnered attention for their superior performance in terms of water permeability and solute rejection. These membranes are generally built with a porous base topped by a selective polyamide layer.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: nce in terms of water permeability and solute rejection. These membranes are generally built with a porous base topped by a selective polyamide layer. This layer is created through interfacial polymerization (IP), which uses an acyl chloride monomer in the organic phase and a diamine monomer in the aqueous phase [43–51]. Nevertheless, the vigorous nature of the IP re­action often leads to an uneven polyamide layer with varying thickness and density [52,53], which can introduce defects or irregularities, ulti­mately resulting in inconsistent separation performance [54,55]. To mitigate this issue, researchers have explored various approaches to fine-tune the polyamide layer’s architecture by regulating the IP process [56–62]. For instance, Zhang et al. developed an electro-sprayed poly­amide nanofiltration membrane with adjustable pores, achieving sub-nm precision molecule separation and demonstrating a facile strategy for fabricating membranes with enhanced selectivity [59]. Despite these innovations, the membrane technology sector

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: Fig. 2. SEM analysis of the TFC membranes subjected to various CE15 post-treatment concentrations. The images depict both surface morphology and cross-sectional views for: (a) control membrane, (b) mC0, (c) mC0.5, (d) mC1, (e) mC2, and (f) mC4. C.-M. Chang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 continues to grapple with the challenge of concurrently enhancing water flux and selectivity while ensuring long-term durability. Some recent investigations have shifted the focus towards post-fabrication treat­ments, such as organic solvent-based post-treatment, which has shown promise in improving membrane performance due to its straightforward application and scalability potential. This technique, independent of the IP process, can enhance water permeability by altering the polyamide layer’s microstructure [63,64]. Additionally, nanoparticles facilitating rapid aqueous transport or exhibiting molecular sieving properties have also been incorporated into membranes, with great potential in breaking through the conventional permeability-selectivity trade-off as well as offering enhanced flexibility and customization in membrane design [65,66]. To further enhance the usability and performance of TFC mem­branes, scientists have explored post-treatment techniques following the fabrication of TFC membranes. A study utilizing dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as a solvent to activate TFC membranes pioneered the research on solvent activation [67].

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: f TFC membranes. A study utilizing dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as a solvent to activate TFC membranes pioneered the research on solvent activation [67]. However, the rejection of TFC membranes activated with DMSO was not ideal, highlighting the need for alternative solvents with better activation outcomes. In recent studies, methanol (MeOH) has proven to be a particularly effective activation solvent for post-treatment of membranes. Among common monohydric alcohols, MeOH treatment has shown the greatest enhancement in water perme­ability without considerably compromising selectivity [68]. This enhancement is attributed to MeOH’s ability to induce controlled swelling of the polyamide layer, thereby optimizing its microstructure for more efficient water transport. Furthermore, MeOH’s industrial versatility and potential for sustainable production, such as through renewable methods or carbon emission utilization, add to its appeal as a preferred choice in membrane post-treatment processes [69–73]. Building upon these findings, Wu et al. demonstrated a method to

C.-M. Chang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 modify the membrane microarchitecture by incorporating nano­materials with the post-treatment strategy [74]. They introduced 4-sul­focalix [4]arene (SCA4), a macromolecule with an inner cavity, into the MeOH activation medium, allowing its integration into the polyamide matrix. A remarkable 129 % increase in water permeability was re­ported, while retaining a high salt rejection of 98.8 %. This study further validated the efficacy of MeOH as an activation solvent, particularly when being combined with cyclic molecules like SCA4. However, it is crucial to note that despite the initial boost in water permeability, the surface modifications using SCA4 and MeOH cannot sustain over long-term use, leading to diminishing water permeability. It underscores the need to carefully balance the short-term performance enhancement with the long-term structural stability of the membranes. To address these challenges, we propose a post-treatment approach

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: Fig. 5. High-resolution XPS (a) O1s spectra and (b) C1s spectra of TFC membranes after MeOH-based post-treatment incorporating different concentrations of CE15. C.-M. Chang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 by inserting crown ether as molecular supports into the polyamide matrix using an activation solvent. CE15, a versatile compound compatible with both polar and non-polar solvents, is dissolved in MeOH for the post-treatment of TFC membranes, leveraging the MeOH’s swelling effect on the polyamide layer. Our study further investigates the impact of CE15 chelated with metal ions, specifically Li+ and Na+ [75–77] on water permeance and structural robustness [78–80]. We hypothesize that the swelling effect of MeOH facilitates the integration of CE15 into the polyamide matrix, where CE15 interacts with amide groups in the selective layer, leading to an optimized microstructure to enhance the water transport and yet preserve a critical selectivity. Additionally, chelation of CE15 with Li+ or Na+ introduces intermo­lecular electrostatic repulsion, serving as molecular supports that rein­force the membrane against performance deterioration under high-pressure compaction over time [53,78–80]. The dissolution of CE15 in MeOH also simplifies the handling and storage processes, potentially improving the efficiency and scalability of membrane pro­duction.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: tion of CE15 in MeOH also simplifies the handling and storage processes, potentially improving the efficiency and scalability of membrane pro­duction. This study provides a facile and scalable method for the post-treatment of TFC membranes to achieve improved performance and long-time stability, advancing the technology for desalination and water purification.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

2. Experimental setup 2.1. Materials CE15 (98 %), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99 %), and lithium nitrate (LiNO3, 99.99 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. MeOH and n-hexane (AR grade) were supplied by Fisher Chemical. PSf substrates were sourced from Aquaporin Asia Pte. Ltd. and stored in ultrapure water until use. Trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98 %) was acquired from Alfa Aesar, while m-phenylenediamine (MPD, 98 %) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. 2.2. Membrane preparations The TFC membranes were produced using a basic procedure [81] as illustrated in Fig. 1. A solution comprising 2 wt% MPD and 0.15 wt% SDS was utilized as the aqueous phase for substrate soaking. The following steps included removing excess solution with a rubber roller, immersing the substrate in an organic phase of 0.15 wt% TMC in hexane for 1 min, air drying naturally for 1 min, and curing in an oven at 60 ◦C for 5 min. After curing, the membranes were air-cooled for 5 min. These

Fig. 6. (a) Membrane performance associated with MeOH-based post-treatment with varied CE15 concentrations. (b) 72-h long-term RO analysis for the untreated control, mC0, and mC1 membranes.

content: Fig. 7. (a) Schematic illustration of MeOH-CE15 solvent activation and diffusion into the polyamide layer. Membrane structural representation of (b) mC0, (c) mC1, and (d) mC4 with increasing CE15 concentration. C.-M. Chang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 as-prepared membranes were denoted as "control" in the subsequent results section. For post-treatment, the control membranes were submerged in MeOH solutions with different CE15 concentrations (i.e., 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 wt%) for 5 min. The 5-min duration was determined through preliminary testing as the optimal time to achieve the best membrane performance. Relevant experimental results are presented in Table S1. Finally, the membranes were air-dried and stored in ultrapure water. These modified membranes were labeled as mCx, where ’m’ indicates post-treatment with MeOH and ’x’ specifies the addition of x wt% CE15. For instance, membranes submerged in MeOH solution without CE15 (0 wt%) are denoted as mC0, while those submerged in MeOH solution containing 1 wt% CE15 are denoted as mC1. To evaluate the impact of Li and Na ion on the physicochemical properties and RO performance of TFC membranes, two more posttreatment solutions were prepared and used: 1 wt% CE15 with equi­molar amount of LiNO3 or NaCl in MeOH. The customized membranes were designated as mC1Li and mC1Na, respectively. 2.3.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: and used: 1 wt% CE15 with equi­molar amount of LiNO3 or NaCl in MeOH. The customized membranes were designated as mC1Li and mC1Na, respectively. 2.3. Characterizations The morphology of the membrane surfaces and cross-sections was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM7610FPlus). To assess the distribution of metallic ions on the mem­brane surface, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was utilized. An atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension ICON) was employed to measure surface roughness, operating in tapping mode under ambient conditions with a 10 * 10 μm2 scan area. The root mean square (RMS) surface roughness was determined by averaging measurements from five distinct locations on each sample. A goniometer (DataPhysics In­struments, OCA25) was used to measure the water contact angle (WCA) of each sample using the standard static droplet technique. The struc­tural characteristics of CE15 membrane were analyzed using an atten­uated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (ATRFTIR, Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan, IR Prestige-21), scanning from 500 to 4000 cm-1 wavenumbers, with 40 scans at a resolution of 4 cm-1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS UltraDLD) was employed for elemental analysis of the selective layer. A zeta potential analyzer (Anton Paar SurPASS 3) was used to systematically characterize surface charge properties with varied pH.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: he selective layer. A zeta potential analyzer (Anton Paar SurPASS 3) was used to systematically characterize surface charge properties with varied pH. The concentration of total organic carbon (TOC) in both the feed solution and permeate was quantified using a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan, TOC-L). 2.4. Separation performance evaluations The separation performance was evaluated using a cross-flow filtration setup. The system was operated with a feed stream flowing at 1 L min-1 across an active membrane region of 15 cm2. For the RO tests, a saline solution containing NaCl of 2000 ppm served as the feed, with its temperature regulated at 25 ± 1 ◦C. All experimental runs were conducted with pressure at 20 ± 1 bar. The permeance, denoted as Pw (L m-2 h-1 bar-1, LMH bar-1), was determined using the following equation [81]: Pw = ΔV/ (A * Δt * ΔP) (1) In this equation, Δt (h) represents the duration of filtration, ΔV (L) is the volume of permeate collected, A (m2) denotes the effective membrane area, and ΔP (bar) signifies the pressure differential across the mem­brane. The salt rejection efficiency, Rs (%), was computed using the formula [81,82]: Rs = (1 -Cp / Cf) * 100 % (2)

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: Fig. 8. Surface, cross-sectional SEM images and AFM images of (a) mC1Li and (b) mC1Na. C.-M. Chang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 where Cf and Cp (mol L-1) represent the salt concentrations in the feed and permeate solutions, respectively. 3. Result and discussion 3.1. Characterizations of CE15-incorporated membranes The relationship between the concentration of the additive CE15 and the morphology of the modified TFC membrane was systematically investigated. High-resolution SEM was employed to examine both the surface texture and cross-sectional structure of the membranes. The results at different CE15 concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 2a–f. All TFC membranes display a characteristic topographical structure featuring ridges and valleys. Notably, treatment with MeOH gives rise to a significant increase in surface roughness (see the AFM results in Fig. 3a–f, h), which is attributed to the dissolution of polyamide oligo­mers. The highest surface roughness is observed at a CE15 concentration of 1 wt%. Based on the cross-sectional SEM images, we measured the membrane thickness as a function of the CE15 concentration (Fig. 3g). For TFC membranes post-treated with MeOH alone, the thickness decreased from 290.1 to 255.8 nm, which was attributed to the disso­lution of surface polyamide oligomers.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: post-treated with MeOH alone, the thickness decreased from 290.1 to 255.8 nm, which was attributed to the disso­lution of surface polyamide oligomers. With the addition of CE15 to MeOH, this dissolution effect was intensified, reaching its maximum at a CE15 concentration of 1 wt%, where the membrane thickness reduced significantly to 193.2 nm. This pronounced reduction may be attributed to the amphiphilic nature of CE15, which could facilitate MeOH pene­tration into the polyamide network. While MeOH alone primarily in­teracts with the hydrophilic regions of polyamide, CE15 might enhance its transport and access to the hydrophobic domains. This interaction could potentially expand the swelling effect throughout the membrane and further promote the dissolution of polyamide oligomers. It also leads to the increased roughness (Fig. 3h). However, as the CE15 concentra­tion increased further, the thickness of the polyamide layer rose and reached 230.6 nm at 4 wt% CE15, and its roughness dropped. Moreover, more CE15 is located near the layer surface (see Fig. S1). This recovery seems to suggest an optimal CE15 concentration (around 1 wt%) for maximizing the MeOH effect. Fig. 3i presents the measured water contact angles (WCA) for sam­ples at different CE15 concentrations. The hydrophilicity of the mem­brane is found to progressively increase as the CE15 concentration rises, reaching its peak at 1 wt% [64,83–85].

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: ations. The hydrophilicity of the mem­brane is found to progressively increase as the CE15 concentration rises, reaching its peak at 1 wt% [64,83–85]. However, beyond 1 wt%, the surface roughness begins to decrease, and the water contact angle in­creases. The observed changes in WCA and surface roughness are consistent with the Wenzel model [86]. The trend in surface roughness also aligns well with our previous study [78]. FTIR spectra, depicted in Fig. 4, reveal distinct peaks at 1548 cm-1 and 1659 cm-1, ascribed to the C=O stretching and N–H in-plane

Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 bending vibrations of polyamide, respectively [53,87–89]. The intro­duction of CE15 is marked by three new peaks at 2965, 1085 and 996 cm-1, attributed to the –CH2 and C–O–C stretching of CE, respectively [90,91]. A shift of the C=O stretching vibration peak from 1659 to 1662 cm-1 can be credited to the hydrogen bonding interaction between CE15 and the amine groups [52], indicating that CE15 molecules are prefer­ably located around these groups. Collectively, the observed alterations in the spectral profile provide strong evidence for the effective inte­gration of CE15 into the structural framework of the membrane. To investigate the polyamide layer’s chemical composition, XPS was employed. The atomic concentrations of each membrane are tabulated in Table 1. Notably, there is an increase in oxygen content and the O/N ratio after post-treating the membrane with CE15-infused MeOH, indicating enhanced integration of CE15. An in-depth analysis of highresolution XPS spectra focusing on O1s and C1s was conducted for both unmodified and CE-treated membranes. Fig.

content: n of CE15. An in-depth analysis of highresolution XPS spectra focusing on O1s and C1s was conducted for both unmodified and CE-treated membranes. Fig. 5, Tables S2 and S3reveal the decomposition of distinct peaks within the O1s spectrum: (1) N–C=O*/O–C=O* (OI, BE = 531.1 eV), (2) H-*O–C/C-*O–C (OII, BE =532.2 eV), and (3) *O–C=O (OIII, BE = 533.3 eV) [52,53,78,92]. Fig. S1a and b document the variations in peak area ratios across different CE15 concentrations, noting a particular increase in the OII/OI ratio. This ratio is considered a dependable marker for the CE15 pres­ence due to the MeOH post-treatment with CE15, indicating enhanced CE15 integration into the polyamide structure. Similarly, analysis was also conducted for the C1s spectrum, revealing alterations in the following peaks: (1) *C–C/*C–H (CI, BE = 284.6 eV), (2) *C-OR (CII, BE

content: = 285.4 eV), (3) *C–N (CIII, BE = 287.8 eV), and (4) *C=O (CIV, BE =288.3 eV) [52,53,78,92]. As illustrated in Fig. S2c, the observed increase in CII after MeOH treatment with CE15 further supports the enhanced insertion of CE15 within the membrane structure. 3.2. Performance evaluation of CE15-incorporated membranes The RO performance of TFC membranes was assessed by employing a lab-scale filtration setup. The results of permeance and rejection are displayed in Fig. 6. The control TFC membrane exhibits a water per­meance of 1.86 LMH bar-1 and a salt rejection of 99.0 %. In contrast, the control group, which is post-treated with neat MeOH, shows an improved permeance of 3.08 LMH bar-1 and a compromised rejection of 97.21 %, in line with related research [74]. Incorporating 1 wt% CE15 into the polyamide layer significantly enhanced water permeance, achieving 4.61 LMH bar-1 while main­taining a rejection rate of 98.9 %. This remarkable 148 % increase in water permeance is due to the synergistic effects of a thinner polyamide layer, improved surface hydrophilicity and an optimized membrane structure. The unique molecular structure of CE15, featured with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties [93,94], ensures its compati­bility with methanol and the polyamide layer [83,94]. The hydrophi­licity of CE15 arises from its cyclic structure containing five oxygen atoms with lone pairs of electrons, facilitating interactions with meth­anol as well as the amide groups of polyamides.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: ructure containing five oxygen atoms with lone pairs of electrons, facilitating interactions with meth­anol as well as the amide groups of polyamides. Conversely, the outer moiety of the CE15 ring primarily consists of carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen bonds, making it hydrophobic and capable of inter­acting with the phenyl segments of polyamide [52,95]. During the post-treatment process, the polyamide layer undergoes structural changes when immersed in a CE15-containing methanol solution for 5 min followed by air-drying [64,83–85]. This process allows the diffusion of CE15 and methanol into the PA layer, while some dissolved oligomers simultaneously diffuse out into the bulk solution, as demonstrated in Fig. 7a. Without CE15, the swelling effect of methanol alone typically compromises rejection performance by altering the polyamide layer’s structure. However, owing to the Janus-like properties, CE15 can remain embedded within the polyamide layer even after methanol evaporation, ensuring structural stability and effectively mitigating rejection deteri­oration. It is noteworthy that higher concentrations of CE15 can lead to excessive accumulation, which blocks the water channels and increases the polyamide layer thickness, as shown in Fig. 7d, ultimately reducing the water permeance.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: cumulation, which blocks the water channels and increases the polyamide layer thickness, as shown in Fig. 7d, ultimately reducing the water permeance. Long-term RO tests on mC1 over 72 h demon­strated its ability to sustain a water permeance of 3.88 LMH bar-1 at the end of the test (15.8 % reduction), whereas the control and mC0 membranes showed the declines to 1.57 and 1.81 LMH bar-1 (15.6 % and 41.2 % reduction), respectively. These results underscore CE15’s role in enhancing the structural stability of the polyamide layer, high­lighting its potential for long-term membrane applications. To address whether CE15 can be stably retained in the membranes after long-term RO tests, TOC measurements were performed on both the feed solution and permeate collected after one week of RO operation. The results are shown in Table S4, indicative of negligible TOC con­centrations. It hence confirmed that CE15 did not leach into either the feed solution or the permeate during the tests. 3.3. Characterizations of mC1Li and mC1Na membranes To further improve the long-term RO performance of mC1, which already demonstrated a reduced water flux decline compared to that without CE15, we explored the chelation strategy using alkali metal cations, Li+ and Na+, due to their significant contributions to membrane stability observed previously [78]. CE15, featuring a cavity size between approximately 1.7 and 2.2 Å, is ideal for chelation with Li+ (1.36 Å) and Na+ (1.94 Å).

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: bserved previously [78]. CE15, featuring a cavity size between approximately 1.7 and 2.2 Å, is ideal for chelation with Li+ (1.36 Å) and Na+ (1.94 Å). Hence, Li+ or Na+ was added into the CE15-MeOH solu­tion for the post-treatment of fabricated TFC membranes, followed by evaluating their physiochemical properties and separation performance. Surface zeta potentials were measured at various pH to examine the effect of metal ion incorporation on the surface charge of the mem­branes. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the membranes with post-treatment using methanol containing CE15 and either Li+ or Na+ exhibit a reduction in negative charge compared to mC1. This observation can be attributed to the positive charge of chelated cations that exist in the membrane and thereby partially neutralize the negative charge due to carboxyl groups. SEM surface images, presented in Fig. 8, show that membranes with the addition of Li+ or Na+ retain a similar level of irregularity and roughness as mC1. Fig. 9 shows the EDX mapping of mC1Na. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124005 is evenly distributed on the surface, once again proving the successful chelation of metal ions with CE15 that is imbedded into the polyamide layer. At the same time, the cross-sectional EDX mapping in Fig. 9b also shows that the Na element successfully penetrated into the membrane interior, which can be considered as evidence that CE15 chelated with Na also entered the membrane. To verify our earlier assertion that MeOH facilitates the introduction of CE15 into the membrane, we compared the cross-sectional EDX mapping of mC1Na membrane with that from a membrane which was purposefully treated with deionized water containing 1 wt% Na chelated CE 15 (denoted as wC1Na). As shown in Fig. S4, the distribution of Na (red dots) for mC1Na in Fig. S4b is denser and more widespread than in Fig. S4a for wC1Na, manifesting MeOH’s greater ability to bring CE15 into the selective layer. Statistical analyses for polyamide thickness, surface roughness and water contact angles are summarized in Fig. 9c–e.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: g CE15 into the selective layer. Statistical analyses for polyamide thickness, surface roughness and water contact angles are summarized in Fig. 9c–e. The membranes of mC1Li and mC1Na exhibit the similar thickness, roughness and contact angles to mC1, indicating that the alkali cations barely alter the mem­brane morphologies or physiochemical properties. Therefore, the exis­tence of Li+ or Na+ is not anticipated to have a significant impact on the water permeability and salt rejection of the membranes. XPS analysis was performed to detect changes in the chemical composition of CE15 membranes when Li+ or Na+ was added. The atomic concentrations, presented in Table 2, show that the oxygen contents and O/N ratios in the membranes of mC1Li and mC1Na are similar to those of mC1. The analyses of O1s and C1s spectra for mem­branes with Li+ or Na+ additions are shown in Fig. 10, Table S5, and Table S6. The variations in peak area, plotted in Fig. S5, reveal that the OIII/OI and OII/OI ratios are in accordance with those of mC1. The loading of CE15 and the degree of cross-linking in the polyamide layer remain consistent with those in the mC1 membrane, proving again the introduction of metal cations does not affect the composition and structure of membrane.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: 3.4. Performance evaluation of mC1Li and mC1Na membranes Fig. 11a presents the results of the RO tests. The inclusion of Li+ and Na+ resulted in water permeances of 4.62 and 4.64 LMH bar-1, respectively, which are nearly identical to that of mC1. Similarly, mC1Li and mC1Na achieve slightly enhanced salt rejections of 99.1 % and 99.3 %, respectively, compared to 98.9 % for mC1. As shown in Fig. 11b, the mC1Li and mC1Na membranes maintained water permeances of 4.31 and 4.48 LMH bar-1, representing only minor reductions of 6.7 % and 3.4 %, respectively, after a 72-h RO test. This stable separation performance contrasts sharply with the rapid decline in water flux observed for the unchelated mC1 membrane, underscoring the enhanced stability of membranes modified with Li+ or Na+. The improved stability can be attributed to the synergistic effects of chela­tion between CE15 and alkali metal. The formed complex likely serves as a rigid molecular support within the polyamide matrix, reinforcing the membrane structure against compaction under continuous highpressure operation. Similar improvement in long-term stability was observed in our previous study when CE15 and alkaline metal ions were added in MPD solution for interfacial polymerization [78]. Tables 3–5provide a comprehensive performance comparison between our TFC membranes and those reported in other studies.

citation.title: Solvent-assisted insertion of molecular supports for enhanced separation performance and stability of thin film composite reverse osmosis membranes

content: terfacial polymerization [78]. Tables 3–5provide a comprehensive performance comparison between our TFC membranes and those reported in other studies. Table 3 demonstrates that our membranes outperform others subjected to various post-treatment processes, offering enhanced separation efficiency. Table 4 shows that CE15-incorporated membranes deliver better overall performance than comparable membranes. Lastly, Table 5 highlights the remarkable long-term stability of our membranes, underscoring their potential for consistent operations in practical water treatment appli­cations. Overall, the membranes developed in the present study set a promising benchmark for water treatment technology owing to their excellent performance and long-term durability.
